cataloging_subject.label: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule
cataloging_subject.scope_note: One anonymous Middle English body-and-soul debate poem represented in CME00130 by four separately labeled witness streams: Auchinleck, Laud Misc. 108, Vernon, and Digby 102. The four DIV2 elements are serialized manuscript versions from Linow's parallel edition, not four independent works or intrinsic chapters of one text.

catalog_summary.title: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Composed by the early fourteenth century at the latest; first attested in a late-thirteenth- or early-fourteenth-century witness
catalog_summary.region: England; represented copies have London, East Anglian, and Worcestershire/West Midlands production or dialect associations

titles.value: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule

assertion.notes: A cataloging title based on the Auchinleck rubric and Linow's title; it is not asserted as authorial.

titles.value: Þe desputisoun bitven þe bodi and þe soule
A disputacion bytwene þe bodi and þe soule
As I lay in a winters night / In a darkening before the day

scope.description: DIMEV's normalized opening across the witness versions

assertion.notes: Each witness has its own spelling and, in places, wording; this normalized form is an index incipit, not a diplomatic transcription.

titles.value: Explicit disputacio inter corpus et animam

agents.name: Wilhelm Linow
notes: Editor, collator, and commentator of the four Middle English witness texts printed in 1889 and represented by CME00130.

agents.name: Hermann Varnhagen
notes: Prepared Linow's working transcripts of the Auchinleck and Digby versions; separately edited the Old French appendix outside CME00130's encoded range.

agents.name: Mr. Parker (Oxford; identity unresolved)
notes: Linow credits this person with arranging or preparing the Laud and Vernon working transcripts. No fuller identity is supplied in the edition.

assertion.notes: No named Middle English author is supported. Attributions associated with other Latin or vernacular body-and-soul debates are not transferred to this poem.

scope.description: The four witness texts on printed pp. 24-105
Reconstructed stanza order, cross-witness emendation, notes, and Simeon variants in the 1889 edition
German introduction and notes on printed pp. 1-23 and 107-112, outside CME00130's encoded text
Working transcript used by Linow for the Auchinleck text
Working transcript used by Linow for the Digby text
Working transcript used by Linow for the Laud text

assertion.notes: The role is explicit; the transcriber's fuller identity remains unresolved.

scope.description: Working transcript used by Linow for the Vernon text

assertion.notes: The role is explicit; the transcriber's fuller identity remains unresolved.

date_statements.display: By the early fourteenth century at the latest

assertion.notes: The Laud witness establishes circulation by this outer bound; no authorial copy or exact composition date survives.

date_statements.display: Late thirteenth or early fourteenth century (Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108)

assertion.notes: The NLS headnote gives c. 1275-1325. The current Bodleian description dates the codex's core to the late thirteenth or early fourteenth century, gives 1290-1400 as the broader machine-readable range, and says the work's Hand B is somewhat later than Hands A and C. This is a witness date, not a composition date.

places.label: East Anglia, England
notes: Used for the Laud Hand B dialect localization to the Isle of Ely or nearby west Norfolk or north-east Suffolk, not for the poem's place of composition.

assertion.notes: This broad cultural origin does not localize composition more narrowly.

scope.description: Production context of the Auchinleck manuscript copy

assertion.notes: The NLS project describes the manuscript as produced in London in the 1330s. This does not establish the poem's original place of composition.

scope.description: Scribal dialect of Laud Hand B

assertion.notes: The current catalog localizes Hand B to the Isle of Ely, or possibly west Norfolk or north-east Suffolk. The uncertainty is preserved at the broader East Anglian level.
The current catalog assigns Scribe 2 to Worcestershire, Linguistic Atlas profile LP 7630. This is a copyist's dialect, not a composition place.
The current catalog gives London as the codex's origin. The poem is a later addition within that codex, so the statement is scoped to the surviving copy rather than the abstract work.
Short Latin rubrics and speech labels are embedded paratext rather than a second whole-work language.

scope.description: One prose transition after the Laud witness's stanza 59 and a unique four-line scribal benediction

assertion.notes: DIMEV v1.0 exposes French at record level because of this Laud-only material. The other encoded witness versions and the abstract poem are not thereby made French or generally macaronic.

form_statements.description: A stanzaic dialogue poem, normally in eight-line abab abab stanzas. Linow and CME lineate all four streams as verse; DIMEV reports that Digby 102 writes its version as prose in the manuscript.

assertion.notes: A nocturnal vision frames the body-and-soul dialogue.

summaries.value: In a night vision, a soul confronts its dead body. The two blame one another for their shared sins, recall worldly vanity and bodily decay, and face demonic punishment; the waking narrator closes with an appeal to repentance and divine mercy.

CME00130 serializes the four witness texts that Linow printed on alternating pages: Auchinleck and Laud first, then Vernon and Digby. It excludes Linow's introduction, Simeon variants, textual notes, Old French appendix, and Theodore Martin adaptation.

DIMEV v1.0 groups all seven known witnesses under record 605 / IMEV and NIMEV 351. Its record-level French flag derives from Laud's unique French sentence and benediction; French is therefore recorded only for the Laud version here.

content_parts.label: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule
content_parts.summary: The anonymous body-and-soul debate as an abstract work, represented below by four parallel manuscript-version parts whose extents and source loci differ.

locators.value: BODY/DIV1 containing four witness-labeled DIV2 streams

notes: The child parts are witness representations for scoping metadata and lineage; they contribute zero additional abstract works to the work-level item count.

content_parts.label: Auchinleck manuscript version
content_parts.summary: Linow's reordered and lightly emended printing of the 74-stanza Auchinleck version. The surviving manuscript ends on a mutilated f. 35ra stub, leaving only the beginnings of lines in the final five stanzas.

locators.value: DIV2[1]; 76 LG and 594 L elements, including two one-line blank alignment placeholders

notes: The 592 figure counts nonempty XML L elements and agrees with DIMEV v1.0; the NLS headnote counts 591 lines while excluding Latin speech labels and the English title. The one-line discrepancy remains unresolved.
This is a witness representation used for part-scoped description and lineage, not an intrinsic division of the abstract poem.

content_parts.label: Laud Misc. 108 version
content_parts.summary: Linow's reordered and lightly emended printing of the 61-stanza Laud version, followed by its unique Old French prose transition and four-line scribal benediction.

locators.value: DIV2[2]; 80 LG and 510 L elements, including eighteen one-line blank alignment placeholders; one prose P between LG 73 and 74

notes: The metrics count 61 eight-line Middle English stanzas plus one four-line Old French verse group; the intervening French prose sentence is not counted as a verse line.
This is a witness representation used for part-scoped description and lineage, not an intrinsic division of the abstract poem.

content_parts.label: Vernon manuscript version
content_parts.summary: Linow's reordered and lightly emended printing of the 62-stanza Vernon version, beginning in the third column of f. 286r and ending in the second column of f. 287r.

locators.value: DIV2[3]; 79 LG and 513 L elements, including seventeen one-line blank alignment placeholders

notes: Linow's printed heading and the XML preserve the legacy locator 'Bl. 285 v.'; the current Bodleian component record and direct images place the work at ff. 286rc-287rb.
This is a witness representation used for part-scoped description and lineage, not an intrinsic division of the abstract poem.

content_parts.label: Digby 102 version
content_parts.summary: Linow's reordered and lightly emended printing of the 67-stanza Digby version. The manuscript presents the poem as prose, while Linow and the XML restore verse lineation.

locators.value: DIV2[4]; 76 LG and 545 L elements, including nine one-line blank alignment placeholders
ff. 136r-139v; no complete public facsimile found

notes: Linow's descriptive paragraph says f. 136v, while his printed text heading, the XML heading, DIMEV, and the current Bodleian catalog give f. 136r. The current locus is used.
This is a witness representation used for part-scoped description and lineage, not an intrinsic division of the abstract poem.

extent.description: One poem in four parallel witness versions. The XML has one DIV1, four DIV2 streams, 311 LG, 2,162 L, 82 PB, and 840 MILESTONE elements. Of the 2,162 L elements, 2,128 are nonempty textual lines and 34 are one-line blank alignment placeholders. Version extents are recorded separately because summing parallel copies would misstate the length of the abstract work: Auchinleck 74 stanzas / 592 encoded nonempty lines; Laud 61 eight-line Middle English stanzas plus a four-line Old French benediction / 492 verse lines; Vernon 62 stanzas / 496 lines; Digby 67 stanzas / 536 lines.

related_works.title: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule
notes: CME00101 encodes Varnhagen's 1879 printing of the Royal MS 18 A X version of the same DIMEV 605 work. It is a separate corpus representation, not an additional intellectual work contained in CME00130.

evidence.summary: The repository source identifies CME00130 and encodes four witness-labeled DIV2 streams with Linow page breaks, stanza headings, verse lines, embedded italics, and parallel line-number milestones.

evidence.citation: Direct structural and textual inspection of CME00130.xml
evidence.summary: Inspection counted one DIV1, four DIV2, 311 LG, 2,162 L, 82 PB, 840 MILESTONE, and 314 HEAD elements. Per-version nonempty L counts are 592, 492, 496, and 536; the remaining 34 L elements sit in one-line blank LG placeholders used to maintain Linow's parallel alignment. The Laud stream also contains one Old French prose P and a four-line Old French LG.

evidence.citation: Wilhelm Linow, Þe Desputisoun bitwen þe Bodi and þe Soule, Erlanger Beiträge zur englischen Philologie 1 (Erlangen and Leipzig: A. Deichert'sche Verlagsbuchhandlung Nachf. [Georg Böhme], 1889)
evidence.locator: Preface pp. v-vi; introduction pp. 1-23; witness texts pp. 24-105; Simeon variants p. 106; notes pp. 107-112
evidence.summary: The title page and preface identify the edition and contributors. Linow explains that he abandoned a synthetic critical text, used Varnhagen's transcripts for Auchinleck and Digby and Parker's for Laud and Vernon, printed the four witness texts on facing alternating pages, reordered stanzas to his reconstructed sequence, supplied manuscript stanza and verse numbers in parentheses, and corrected obvious sense-disrupting readings through comparison with other witnesses.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, record 605, IMEV/NIMEV 351
evidence.locator: Record 605 and its seven witness entries
evidence.summary: DIMEV groups seven witnesses under one record, identifies the poem as a body-and-soul dialogue in 61-74 eight-line abab abab stanzas, gives exact current loci for the four CME00130 witnesses, notes that Digby is written as prose, and attributes the record's French content to Laud's unique four-line benediction.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, Zenodo record 14585961, DOI 10.5281/zenodo.14585961
evidence.summary: The exact v1.0 dataset is cached with fixity. Its ZIP comment identifies Git commit 5ad5f592868471b44fc72f6e2f42db419780b42c, and its LICENSE grants CC BY 4.0.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland Auchinleck Manuscript project, headnote for Þe Desputisoun Bitven þe Bodi and þe Soule
evidence.summary: The headnote identifies Scribe 1, eight-line abab abab stanzas, 591 lines excluding Latin speech labels and the English title, the damaged ending at f. 35ra, six other witnesses with dates and stanza counts, and Linow's edition.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, The Auchinleck Manuscript
evidence.summary: The NLS project dates production to 1331-1340 and describes the manuscript as produced in London in the 1330s; its physical account explains the mutilated f. 35r stub and other losses.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Catalogue of Western Medieval Manuscripts, MS. Laud Misc. 108
evidence.locator: MS_Laud_Misc_108-item2; origin, hand, layout, and history descriptions
evidence.summary: The current component record places the debate at ff. 200v-203v, identifies Hand B, dates the codex core to the late thirteenth or early fourteenth century with additions, and localizes Hand B's dialect to the Isle of Ely or possibly west Norfolk or north-east Suffolk.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Catalogue of Western Medieval Manuscripts, MS. Eng. poet. a. 1 (Vernon Manuscript)
evidence.summary: The current component record places the debate at ff. 286rc-287rb, gives its rubric, incipit, explicit, and Middle English language, dates the manuscript c. 1390-1400, and localizes Scribe 2's dialect to Worcestershire (LP 7630).

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Catalogue of Western Medieval Manuscripts, MS. Digby 102
evidence.locator: Component at ff. 136r-139v; origin and physical description
evidence.summary: The current component record places the debate at ff. 136r-139v, transcribes its rubric, incipit, and final rubric, identifies it as a later addition in different ink with a separate decoration scheme, and dates the London parchment codex to 1425-1450.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's composition be localized or dated more narrowly than circulation by the early fourteenth century in England?
next_steps: Review recent work on the seven-witness tradition and its dialectal reconstruction.
Keep copyist dialects and codex production places scoped to individual witness versions unless scholarship establishes authorial origin.

open_questions.question: Why does the NLS headnote count 591 Auchinleck lines while DIMEV v1.0 and the nonempty CME line elements count 592?
next_steps: Compare line-by-line counting conventions in the NLS transcription, Linow pp. 24-64, and the damaged final five stanzas.
Preserve both attributed counts until the exact divergent line is identified.

notes: The 840 MILESTONE elements are interleaved editorial and manuscript line-number systems, often duplicated at the same point; they are not additional verse lines.

Blank one-line LG elements preserve the vertical relationship of Linow's facing witness texts after the U-M encoding serialized the page streams. They are alignment machinery, not missing or empty stanzas in the abstract poem.
Current repository component records control manuscript loci. Linow's historical folio labels are preserved in part notes and lineage rather than silently normalized.
